html:
Full Tear-Off Roof Replacement: What Gets Removed and What Gets Verified

Tear-off replacement means the entire existing roof assembly comes off down to the structural deck, every layer gets inspected, and a new system goes on with nothing hidden underneath it. It costs more than a recover and takes longer, but it's the only option once a roof has failed past the point where covering it does any good. Here's what the process actually involves and where owners get shorted on scope.

When Recover Isn't an Option Anymore

Some roofs reach a point where layering a new membrane on top just seals a problem in place. Trapped moisture that's saturated the insulation, a deck that's rusted or rotted enough to need structural attention, or a building that's already carrying two roof layers and can't legally take a third — any of those pushes the job from recover to full tear-off.

We see this most on older commercial buildings around downtown Springfield and along the established retail corridors, where a roof may have been recovered once already in the 1990s or 2000s and is now past the point a third layer would hold up.

Building use factors into the decision too. A distribution operation near the I-44 interchanges that can't tolerate water tracking over palletized inventory has a lower tolerance for a marginal recover than a low-traffic storage building would. We ask how the space is actually used before recommending which path makes sense, weighing that alongside what the roof itself can technically support.

What Comes Off the Roof, and Where It Goes

Tear-off means stripping the membrane, insulation, cover board, and any wet material down to bare deck, then hauling it off site. That's a logistics problem as much as a roofing one — dumpster placement on a site with limited yard space, crane or conveyor selection for a multi-story building, and coordinating haul-away so debris doesn't sit on site through a rain event.

A quote that doesn't specify dumpster count, haul frequency, or how debris is contained during the tear-off window is a quote that hasn't actually planned the job. We spec disposal logistics before the crew shows up, not after.

Deck Verification Once the Roof Is Bare

Steel deck is common on the newer distribution and industrial buildings around the I-44 interchanges. Wood deck and older gypsum deck still show up on retail and office buildings built decades ago. Each one fails differently — steel deck corrodes at fastener penetrations and drain bowls, wood deck rots where moisture has sat longest, and gypsum deck crumbles once it's been wet for any length of time.

Tear-off is the only point in the project where the deck is actually visible. We document it with photos before anything goes back down, price repair by the square foot against what we find rather than a flat allowance, and show the owner the deck before it's covered again.

  • Photos of the bare deck across the full roof area rather than sample sections
  • Fastener pattern and pull-test results if wind uplift rating is a concern
  • Written deck repair pricing tied to actual square footage found, not an estimate
  • Confirmation of insulation R-value and attachment method for the new assembly
  • Disposal manifest showing where tear-off debris was hauled

Scope Language That Hides the Real Job

The tear-off quotes that come in low next to ours usually get there by leaving something out, not by being more efficient. "Tear-off to existing deck" without specifying how many layers are assumed. A disposal line that doesn't say how many loads or where they go. A deck repair allowance that's a round number instead of a per-square-foot rate. We write it the other way — every assumption named, every allowance tied to a rate, so the number an owner signs is the number they actually pay.

It's worth asking any contractor directly what happens if the deck needs more repair than the allowance covers. If the answer is vague, that's the tell.

Sequencing a Tear-Off Around Missouri Weather

An open deck is the most vulnerable point in the entire project. We section tear-off so only as much roof is opened as can be dried in with insulation and a base layer before the end of the day, and we watch the forecast closely through spring and early summer when pop-up storm cells are most likely across the Ozarks. A tear-off crew that opens the whole roof at once because it's faster is trading schedule for risk the owner ends up carrying if weather turns.

We also plan crew size and material staging around that same sequencing. Enough insulation and base membrane has to be on hand and pre-cut before a section opens, because waiting on a material delivery with bare deck exposed is exactly the kind of gap a fast-moving afternoon storm can turn into a real problem. Owners rarely see this part of the plan, but it's the difference between a tear-off that stays dry and one that doesn't.

Tear-Off and Replacement Questions

How do you know if a roof needs tear-off instead of recover?

A core sample tells us if insulation is wet or the deck is compromised. Layer count and local code limits settle the rest.

What happens to the old roofing material?

It's hauled off site to disposal, with the load count and destination documented as part of the project record.

Can you tear off only part of a roof at a time?

Yes, and on most occupied buildings that's exactly how we sequence it, section by section, so no more deck is exposed than can be closed in before end of day.

What if the deck needs more repair than expected?

We stop, photograph the area, and price the additional repair against a per-square-foot rate set before the project started, rather than guessing at it mid-job.

How long does full tear-off take compared to a recover?

Tear-off generally runs longer since deck prep and disposal add steps a recover skips, though the exact difference depends on building size and deck condition.
